speaking_circuit.description: The Wrong Villain series IS a conference keynote. β = 0.061 is the hook. 164 people argued in the comments — that is an engaged audience.
speaking_circuit.herald_action: Identify and pitch 2 speaking opportunities per week. Start with PSHE Association and ASCL — most aligned to the series thesis.

policy_report.description: Wrong Villain posts 1–10 compiled into a PDF policy briefing with full citations.
policy_report.target_buyers: Local Authority safeguarding leads, MAT CEOs, DfE policy team, NSPCC policy team
policy_report.herald_action: Begin pitching to LA safeguarding teams once series reaches 8 posts. Herald drafts the pitch letter.

media_credibility.description: 59K impressions, β = 0.061 finding, 46 studies — this is press-ready. The counter-narrative to Haidt and phone bans.
media_credibility.key_angle: UK founder analyses 46 studies and 79 effect sizes. Conclusion: the mental health crisis started in 2008, not 2012. Social media is not the villain policymakers think it is.
media_credibility.secondary_angle: 258 educators saving the post — grassroots evidence that teachers are changing how they talk about screens based on this research synthesis.

pr_asset_data.key_finding: β = 0.061 — social media's effect on youth mental health is statistically indistinguishable from noise across 46 studies and 79 effect sizes

outlets_with_appetite: The Guardian Education — policy and digital childhood
BBC News Education — national policy hooks with a counter-narrative
Schools Week — MAT and policy angles
The i newspaper — accessible science journalism

recent_actions.action: Email this pitch to Dan Milmo and Robert Booth at the Guardian technology desk this morning, headed "Reaction ready: who decides what 'safer' social media means?", with the five-test checklist attached and the founder on standby for the moment the speech lands.

Email this pitch to Robert Booth and Dan Milmo at the Guardian today, subject line "The 90-day clock runs through the school holidays — expert ready on what parents do now," with the founder on standby for same-day comment.

Email this pitch to the Guardian UK news desk (author of the 13 June "what do their children think?" piece) and cc Robert Booth, subject line "The morning-after problem: where under-16s actually go once the ban lands — DSL-level expert on standby today."

recent_questions.question: Has the founder approved going on record advocating a standards-based "safer platform" definition — and are we comfortable being publicly seen to benefit commercially from the carve-out we'd be lobbying for?

Has the founder agreed to be positioned on the emotionally heavy CSAM/sexting beat specifically (not just the "design/literacy" beat), since that determines whether I can lead future pitches with this harder, higher-cut-through news peg?

Can the founder give me 2–3 named, specific examples of the smaller/less-moderated apps children migrated to after the Australian and earlier UK rollouts, so the pitch carries concrete detail rather than a generic "displacement" claim?

notes_to_self: Herald has two jobs: (1) get Justin on stages — speaking is both revenue and top-of-funnel. (2) get the Wrong Villain thesis into press coverage — credibility that no amount of LinkedIn posts can buy. Prioritise speaking circuit first. Each talk = £3K–£8K + hundreds of new GUIDE funnel entries.
